## [00:00 - 03:04]

So in this lesson we're going to look at when is the best time to start expanding your product range and bringing in new additional products to try and grow the market and grow your reach. But in doing that I think it's important to say that like I've said before growth doesn't have to come from new products, it can come from new markets. So before you do a new product you really want to be sure that you've absolutely nailed and mastered your first product because with a new product becomes sometimes a lot more complexity, you're stretched a little thinner and there's a lot more to do and to do your second product as well as your first product can be challenging. Now it depends on your product mix and we'll cover that later on in this lesson. So in my second album framework what I think about from top down is before introducing a new product is firstly as we stated before making sure you've mastered your first product. The next part of that is is this product a new onboarding product which means that product will be pushed to a new market and get new customers in the door or is it a complementary product. Now the difference here is that the new onboarding product there's a lot more to do, you've got to work out a way of getting all these new customers and it's a lot more restrained on your resources. The complementary product is generally you're selling it to the same customers so it's much less strained on your resources and something that's sometimes much easier to plug in but generally probably won't have as big an impact as that new onboarding product so you've got to take that into account and then what we really need to look at is you've definitely validated those markets and validated those products as we discussed previously and we'll also look at the finances of this second album product which is if it's onboarding it may be a little bit more expensive and harder to get off the ground if it's complementary maybe easier to get off the ground and much cheaper and we'll cover that in a preceding lesson. So throughout all of this framework I think a great thing to keep in mind is that that original product the thing that's got you to where you are now has to be not only paying for itself at this stage but also paying for the new innovations, the new products you're going to bring to market and the learning phase of those markets because it's not necessarily that you're going to get a product put it to the market and start making money on the straightaway and what it might be is that you get the product you put it to the market you start learning and then you start making money on it later. Something I see consistently people get wrong at this point in their journey is they're trying to incorporate new products, new SKUs into their lineup and they can accidentally drop the ball on their existing product and what's put them where they are today so you're really going to make sure that your base is strong, your foundation is strong and that everything you're doing is moving the business up, pushing the business forward and not taking away from what you've already built. The reason I call this a second album is because everyone knows that in music this is the hardest time to have a hit so you can have that first thing that goes really well and you do

## [03:04 - 03:39]

really well out of it and you're probably thinking about it for ages, taking heaps of time to get to market but on that second album people can be just trying to throw things at the wall see what sticks and sometimes you can really drop the ball in the first thing so it's don't expect it to be that much easier expect that you have to go in work hard and make it work for you it's not going to make itself work. So at this point you need to fill out your second album framework exercise to work out if now's the best time for you to do your second album do that now and I'll catch you in the next lesson.
